original_hypothesis.title: BOLDUpgradeAction.perform lacks access control: anyone can trigger migrations if ProxyAdmin owners are (mis)configured to allow it
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BOLDUpgradeAction.perform is external and has no access control. If the ProxyAdmin contracts referenced by the immutable addresses are misconfigured such that the caller can execute upgrades (e.g., ProxyAdmin ownership not held by timelock/upgrade executor, or ownership accidentally transferred to BOLDUpgradeAction / EOA), any attacker can call perform() to pause old rollup, upgrade surrounding contracts, and migrate to a new rollup under attacker-influenced timing, potentially causing loss of control or disruption.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While normally the ProxyAdmins are owned by governance, the absence of an explicit access check on perform makes the system brittle to configuration mistakes. Given multiple scripts and complex upgrade orchestration, misconfigurations are realistic and high impact.
deployment_assumptions: At least one of the referenced ProxyAdmin instances allows upgrades by an attacker (ownership mis-set or compromised)
BOLDUpgradeAction is deployed with real ProxyAdmin addresses and not a dry-run
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: BOLDUpgradeAction.sol perform(address[] validators) is external with no modifier; it calls PROXY_ADMIN_*.upgrade/upgradeAndCall which will succeed only if ProxyAdmin permissions allow it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external perform() with no access control; executes ProxyAdmin.upgrade* calls

preconditions: ProxyAdmin permissions are incorrectly configured or compromised
BOLDUpgradeAction has been deployed pointing at production contracts

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no access control on an upgrade orchestration entry point
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: anyone can attempt migration; if ProxyAdmin allows, it will execute

analysis_questions: In intended deployment, who owns each ProxyAdmin at time of perform execution?
Do scripts guarantee ProxyAdmin ownership is timelock-controlled before BOLDUpgradeAction is deployed?
Could a partial misconfiguration allow upgrading some components but not others (leaving system inconsistent)?
validation_requirements: Review prepareBoldUpgrade.ts / deployBoldUpgrade flow to ensure ProxyAdmins remain under governance control
On a fork, attempt calling perform from an EOA with no privileges and confirm it fails due to ProxyAdmin auth
Assess blast radius if only some upgrades succeed (mid-transaction revert behavior should roll back)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls BOLDUpgradeAction.perform; if ProxyAdmins are misowned, the call upgrades bridge stack and deploys a new rollup, causing governance loss or rollup disruption.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential protocol takeover/disruption during migration; could redirect rollup pointers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low if governance control is lost; otherwise medium with corrective upgrades

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting perform is only callable by governance/executor

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor ProxyAdmin owner addresses and ensure they are timelock/executor

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
